question_text: A woman discovered a scratch along a straight line on a circular table top of radius 8 cm. She divided the table top into 4 equal quadrants and discovered the scratch passing through the origin inclined at an angle {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} anticlockwise along the positive direction of x-axis. Find the area of the region enclosed by the {tex}x{/tex} -axis, the scratch and the circular table top in the first quadrant, using integration.
explanation: Equation of the circular tabletop: {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=64{/tex} Equation of line (scratch): {tex}{x}={y}{/tex} The line and circle intersect at {tex}x=4 \sqrt{2}{/tex} Area of the shaded region {tex}=\int_{0}^{4 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_{4 \sqrt{2}}^{8} \sqrt{64-x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left.=\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{4 \sqrt{2}}+\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{64-x^{2}}+32 \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{8}\right]_{4 \sqrt{2}}^{8}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{32}{2}+32 \sin ^{-1} 1-2 \sqrt{2} \cdot 4 \sqrt{2}-32 \sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}=16+16 \pi-16-8 \pi=8 \pi {~cm}^{2}{/tex}
solution: Equation of the circular tabletop: {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=64{/tex} Equation of line (scratch): {tex}{x}={y}{/tex} The line and circle intersect at {tex}x=4 \sqrt{2}{/tex} Area of the shaded region {tex}=\int_{0}^{4 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_{4 \sqrt{2}}^{8} \sqrt{64-x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left.=\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{4 \sqrt{2}}+\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{64-x^{2}}+32 \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{8}\right]_{4 \sqrt{2}}^{8}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{32}{2}+32 \sin ^{-1} 1-2 \sqrt{2} \cdot 4 \sqrt{2}-32 \sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}=16+16 \pi-16-8 \pi=8 \pi {~cm}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of the circular tabletop:
{tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=64{/tex}
Equation of line (scratch): {tex}{x}={y}{/tex}
The line and circle intersect at {tex}x=4 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
Area of the shaded region
{tex}=\int_{0}^{4 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_{4 \sqrt{2}}^{8} \sqrt{64-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}\left.=\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{4 \sqrt{2}}+\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{64-x^{2}}+32 \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{8}\right]_{4 \sqrt{2}}^{8}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{32}{2}+32 \sin ^{-1} 1-2 \sqrt{2} \cdot 4 \sqrt{2}-32 \sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=16+16 \pi-16-8 \pi=8 \pi {~cm}^{2}{/tex}

explanation: The limits of integration are changed. When {tex}x=-1, \sin \theta=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , so {tex}\theta=-\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} . The integral becomes {tex}A=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sqrt{4-(2 \sin \theta)^2}(2 \cos \theta) d \theta{/tex} . The expression under the square root is simplified: {tex} \sqrt{4-4 \sin ^2 \theta}=\sqrt{4\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)}=\sqrt{4 \cos ^2 \theta}=2 \cos \theta {/tex} The integral is now {tex}A=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}}(2 \cos \theta)(2 \cos \theta) d \theta=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} 4 \cos ^2 \theta d \theta{/tex} . The limits are applied: {tex}A=\left(2\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\sin \left(2\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right)\right)-\left(2\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\sin \left(2\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right)\right){/tex}

question_text: Using integration, find the area of the region bounded by the parabola y 2 = 4x, the lines x = 0 and x = 3 and the x-axis.
explanation: Here y 2 = 4x is a right handed parabola having its vertex at the origin and x = 1, x = 4 are the lines parallel to the y-axis at x = 1 to x = 4 units distance. Similarly y 2 = 4x contains even power of y and is symmetrical about the x-axis. So shaded area is the required area Shaded area = {tex}\int_\limits0^3ydx=\int_\limits0^3\sqrt{4x}dx{/tex} = {tex}2\int_\limits0^3\sqrt xdx=2\left[\frac{x^\frac32}{\frac32}\right]_0^3{/tex} = {tex}2\left[\frac{2x^\frac32}3\right]_0^3=2\left[\frac{2\left(3\right)^\frac32}3-0\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[\frac{2\times3\sqrt3}3\right]=4\sqrt3\;{/tex} sq\unit​​​​​​​

explanation: Points in the form of line in the given diagram {tex}{/tex} The equation of side AB is, {tex}y-0=\frac{5-0}{4-2}(x-2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{5}{2}(x-2){/tex} The equation of side BC is, {tex}y-3=\frac{5-3}{4-6}(x-6){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-3=\frac{2}{-2}(x-6){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-3=-1(x-6){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=-x+9{/tex} The equation of side AC is, {tex}y-0=\frac{3-0}{6-2}(x-2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{3}{4}(x-2){/tex} Area {tex} = \frac{5}{2}\int_2^4 {(x - 2)dx + \int_4^6 { - (x - 9)dx - \frac{3}{4}\int_2^6 {(x - 2)dx} } } {/tex} {tex}A = \int_2^4 {\frac{5}{2}(x - 2)dx + \int_0^1 { - (x + 9)dx + \int_6^2 {\frac{3}{4}(x - 2)dx} } } {/tex} On integrating we get, {tex}A = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - 2x} \right]_2^4 + \left[ {\frac{{ - {x^2}}}{2} + 9x} \right]_1^0 - \frac{3}{4}\left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - 2x} \right]_2^6{/tex} On applying limits we get, {tex}A = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {8 - 8 - 2 + 4} \right] + \left[ { - 18 + 54 + 8 - 36} \right] - \frac{3}{4}\left[ {18 - 12 - 2 + 4} \right]{/tex} {tex}A = 5 - 8 - \frac{3}{4}(8){/tex} = 13 - 6 = 7 sq. units. Hence the required area is 7 sq. units.

solution_html:
Points in the form of line in the given diagram

{tex}{/tex}The equation of side AB is,
{tex}y-0=\frac{5-0}{4-2}(x-2){/tex}

On applying limits we get,
{tex}A = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {8 - 8 - 2 + 4} \right] + \left[ { - 18 + 54 + 8 - 36} \right] - \frac{3}{4}\left[ {18 - 12 - 2 + 4} \right]{/tex}

question_text: Find the area of the region lying in the first quadrant and enclosed by the x-axis, the line y = x and the circle x 2 + y 2 = 32.
explanation: The given equations are y = x ... (1) and x 2 + y 2 = 32 ... (2) Solving (1) and (2), we find that the line and the circle meet at B(4, 4) in the first quadrant. Draw perpendicular BM to the x-axis. Therefore, the required area = area of the region OBMO + area of the region BMAB. Now, the area of the region OBMO {tex} = \int_0^4 {ydx = \int_0^4 {xdx} } {/tex} ...(3) {tex} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{x^2}} \right]_0^4 = 8{/tex} Again, the area of the region BMAB {tex} = \int_4^{4\sqrt 2 } {ydx = \int_4^{4\sqrt 2 } {\sqrt {32 - {x^2}} dx} } {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{1}{2}x\sqrt {32 - {x^2}} + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{x}{{4\sqrt2}}} \right]_4^{4\sqrt 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \left( {\frac{1}{2}4\sqrt 2 \times 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}1} \right) - \left( {\frac{4}{2}\sqrt {32 - 16} + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 8\pi - (8 + 4\pi ) = 4\pi - 8{/tex} ...(4) Adding (3) and (4), we get, the required area = {tex}4\pi {/tex} .

{tex} = 8\pi - (8 + 4\pi ) = 4\pi - 8{/tex} ...(4)

question_text: Using integration, find the area of the smaller region bounded by the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 1{/tex} and the line {tex}\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{2} = 1{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 1{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{2} = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{{\left( 3 \right)}^2}}}+\frac{{{y^2}}}{{{{\left( 2 \right)}^2}}} = 1{/tex} is the equation of ellipse and {tex}\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{2} = 1{/tex} is the equation of intercept form of line Area {tex} = \frac{2}{3}\int_0^3 {\sqrt {9 - {x^2}} dx - \frac{2}{3}\int_0^3 {\left( {{{3 - x}}{}} \right)} dx} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{x}{2}\sqrt{9-x^2}+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}\frac{x}{3}-3x+\frac{x^2}{3}} \right]_0^3{/tex} {tex}= \frac{2}{3}\left[ {(0+\frac{9}{2}(sin^{-1}(-1)-3(3)+\frac{9}{2})-0} \right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}[\frac{9 {tex}\pi{/tex} }{4}-\frac{9}{2}]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {\pi - 2} \right){/tex} sq. unit
solution: {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 1{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{2} = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{{\left( 3 \right)}^2}}}+\frac{{{y^2}}}{{{{\left( 2 \right)}^2}}} = 1{/tex} is the equation of ellipse and {tex}\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{2} = 1{/tex} is the equation of intercept form of line Area {tex} = \frac{2}{3}\int_0^3 {\sqrt {9 - {x^2}} dx - \frac{2}{3}\int_0^3 {\left( {{{3 - x}}{}} \right)} dx} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{x}{2}\sqrt{9-x^2}+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}\frac{x}{3}-3x+\frac{x^2}{3}} \right]_0^3{/tex} {tex}= \frac{2}{3}\left[ {(0+\frac{9}{2}(sin^{-1}(-1)-3(3)+\frac{9}{2})-0} \right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}[\frac{9 {tex}\pi{/tex} }{4}-\frac{9}{2}]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {\pi - 2} \right){/tex} sq. unit
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{2} = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{{\left( 3 \right)}^2}}}+\frac{{{y^2}}}{{{{\left( 2 \right)}^2}}} = 1{/tex} is the equation of ellipse and
{tex}\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{2} = 1{/tex} is the equation of intercept form of line
Area {tex} = \frac{2}{3}\int_0^3 {\sqrt {9 - {x^2}} dx - \frac{2}{3}\int_0^3 {\left( {{{3 - x}}{}} \right)} dx} {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{x}{2}\sqrt{9-x^2}+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}\frac{x}{3}-3x+\frac{x^2}{3}} \right]_0^3{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{2}{3}\left[ {(0+\frac{9}{2}(sin^{-1}(-1)-3(3)+\frac{9}{2})-0} \right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}[\frac{9{tex}\pi{/tex}}{4}-\frac{9}{2}]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {\pi - 2} \right){/tex} sq. unit

explanation: {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} {tex} = 4\int_0^a {\frac{b}{a}\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} dx} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{4b}{a} \left[ {\frac{x}{2}\sqrt{a^2-x^2}+\frac{a^2}{2}sin^{-1}(\frac{x}{a})} \right]_0^a{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4b}{a} \left[ {(\frac{a}{2}×0+\frac{a^2}{2}sin^{-1}1)-0} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{4b}{a}\frac{a^2}{2}\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{2}{/tex} {tex} = \pi ab{/tex} sq. units

question_text: Using integration, find the area of the region: {(x, y) : 9x 2 + 4y 2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 36, 3x + y {tex}\geq{/tex} 6}.
explanation: Given region is {(x, y): 9x 2 +4 y 2 {tex}\le{/tex} 36 and 3x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 6} We draw the curves corresponding to equations 9x 2 + 4y 2 = 36 or {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}{/tex} = 1 and 3x + y = 6 The curves intersect at (2, 0) and (0, 6) Shaded area is the area enclosed by the two curves and is given as, = {tex}\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{9\left(1-\frac{x^{2}}{4}\right) d x}{/tex} - {tex}\int_{0}^{2}(6-3 x) d x{/tex} = {tex}3\left[\frac{x}{4} \sqrt{4-x^{2}}\right.{/tex} + {tex}\left.\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}-2 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3\left[\frac{2}{4} \sqrt{4-4}\right.{/tex} + {tex}\left.\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{2}{2}-4+\frac{4}{2}-0\right]{/tex} = {tex}3\left[2 \frac{\pi}{2}-2\right]{/tex} = 3( {tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) square units.
solution: Given region is {(x, y): 9x 2 +4 y 2 {tex}\le{/tex} 36 and 3x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 6} We draw the curves corresponding to equations 9x 2 + 4y 2 = 36 or {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}{/tex} = 1 and 3x + y = 6 The curves intersect at (2, 0) and (0, 6) Shaded area is the area enclosed by the two curves and is given as, = {tex}\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{9\left(1-\frac{x^{2}}{4}\right) d x}{/tex} - {tex}\int_{0}^{2}(6-3 x) d x{/tex} = {tex}3\left[\frac{x}{4} \sqrt{4-x^{2}}\right.{/tex} + {tex}\left.\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}-2 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3\left[\frac{2}{4} \sqrt{4-4}\right.{/tex} + {tex}\left.\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{2}{2}-4+\frac{4}{2}-0\right]{/tex} = {tex}3\left[2 \frac{\pi}{2}-2\right]{/tex} = 3( {tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) square units.
solution_html:
Given region is {(x, y): 9x2 +4 y2 {tex}\le{/tex} 36 and 3x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 6}
We draw the curves corresponding to equations
9x2 + 4y2 = 36 or {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}{/tex} = 1 and 3x + y = 6
The curves intersect at (2, 0) and (0, 6)
Shaded area is the area enclosed by the two curves and is given as,

= {tex}\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{9\left(1-\frac{x^{2}}{4}\right) d x}{/tex} - {tex}\int_{0}^{2}(6-3 x) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}3\left[\frac{x}{4} \sqrt{4-x^{2}}\right.{/tex} + {tex}\left.\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}-2 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}{/tex} 
= {tex}3\left[\frac{2}{4} \sqrt{4-4}\right.{/tex} + {tex}\left.\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{2}{2}-4+\frac{4}{2}-0\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}3\left[2 \frac{\pi}{2}-2\right]{/tex} = 3({tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) square units.

question_text: Using integration, find the area of the triangle whose vertices are (2, 3), (3, 5) and (4, 4).
explanation: Equation of AB : y = 2x - 1 Equation of BC : y = -x + 8 Equation of AC : y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(x+4){/tex} Required Area = {tex}\int\limits_2^3(2 x-1) d x+\int\limits_3^4(-x+8) d x-\int\limits_2^4\left(\frac{x+4}{2}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\left[x^2-x\right]_2^3+\left[\frac{-x^2}{2}+8 x\right]_3^4-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{x^2}{2}+4 x\right]_2^4{/tex} = {tex}4+\frac{9}{2}-7=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Using integration, find the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC whose vertices are A(3, 2), B(5, 7) and C(7, 5).
explanation: Equation of AB: y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(5 x-11){/tex} Equation of BC: y = -x + 12 Equation of AC: y = {tex}\frac{1}{4}(3 x-1){/tex} Required area {tex}=\int_\limits3^5 \frac{1}{2}(5 \mathrm{x}-11) \mathrm{d} x+\int_5^7(-\mathrm{x}+12) \mathrm{d} x-\int_3^7 \frac{1}{4}(3 \mathrm{x}-1) \mathrm{dx}{/tex} {tex}\left.\left.\left.=\frac{1}{2} \frac{(5 x-11)^2}{2 \times 5}\right]_3^5+\frac{(12-x)^2}{-2}\right]_5^7-\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{(3 x-1)^2}{6}\right]_3^7{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{20}(196-16)-\frac{1}{2}(25-49)-\frac{1}{24}(400-64){/tex} = 7

explanation: {tex}4y = 3{x^2}{/tex} ......(1) 2y = 3x + 12......(2) From (2), {tex}y = \frac{{3x + 12}}{2}{/tex} Using this value of y in (1), we get, x 2 - 6x - 8 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (x + 2) (x - 4) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x = -2, 4 From (2), When, x = -2, y = 3 When, x = 4, y = 12 Thus, points of intersection are, (-2, 3) and (4, 12). (ii) in eq.(i) , we get {tex} x ^ { 2 } + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 3 } = 16{/tex} {tex}\frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } { 3 } = 16{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x 2 = 48 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x= {tex}\pm 2 \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} When x= {tex} 2 \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} , then {tex}y = \frac { 2 \sqrt { 3 } } { \sqrt { 3 } } = 2{/tex} Required area (In first quadrant) = ( Area under the line y = {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} x from x = 0 to 2 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} ) + (Area under the circle from x = 2 {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} to x=4 ) {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \sqrt { 3 } } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } x d x + \int _ { 2 \sqrt { 3 } } ^ { 4 } \sqrt { 16 - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} + {tex}\left[ \frac { x } { 2 } \sqrt { 16 - x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { 4 } \right) \right] _ { 2 \sqrt { 3 } } ^ { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { 3 } } \left[ ( 2 \sqrt { 3 } ) ^ { 2 } - 0 \right] + \left[ 0 + 8 \sin ^ { - 1 } (1 )\right.{/tex} {tex}- \frac { 2 \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \sqrt { 16 - 12 } - 8 \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 \sqrt { 3 } } { 4 } \right) ]{/tex} {tex}= 2 \sqrt { 3 } + 8 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) -{/tex} {tex}\frac { 2 \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \times 2 - 8 \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 2 \sqrt { 3 } + 4 \pi - 2 \sqrt { 3 } - 8 \left( \frac { \pi } { 3 } \right){/tex} {tex}= 4 \pi - \frac { 8 \pi } { 3 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 12 \pi - 8 \pi } { 3 } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 \pi } { 3 }{/tex} sq units.

{tex} = \frac { 12 \pi - 8 \pi } { 3 } {/tex}

{tex}=2\left[\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}+\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{t}{2} \sqrt{9-t^{2}}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{3}\right)\right]_{-1}^{3}{/tex}
{tex}=2\left[\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]+\frac{1}{4}\left[\left\{\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{9-(3)^{2}}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{3}\right)\right\}-\left\{\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{9-(1)^{2}}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\right\}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3 \sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{4}\left[\left\{0+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}(1)\right\}-\left\{\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{8}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\right\}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}+\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{9 \pi}{4}-\sqrt{2}-\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}+\frac{9 \pi}{16}-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}-\frac{9}{8} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9 \pi}{16}-\frac{9}{8} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)+\frac{\sqrt{2}}{12}{/tex}
Therefore, the required area is {tex}\left[2 \times\left(\frac{9 \pi}{16}-\frac{9}{8} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)+\frac{\sqrt{2}}{12}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9 \pi}{8}-\frac{9}{4} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} units

solution_html:
According to the question ,

Given equation of circle is {tex}x^2 + y^2 = 4{/tex}......(i)
On differentiating Eq. (i) w.r.t. x , we get
{tex}2x + 2y\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x + y \frac { d } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { x } { y }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) _ { ( 1 , \sqrt { 3 } ) } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore m = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} ( 'm' is slope of tangent )
Now, equation of tangent at point (1, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}) is
{tex}( y - \sqrt { 3 } ) = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } ( x - 1 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt3 y - 3 = -x + 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x + \sqrt { 3 } y = 4{/tex} .......(ii)
equation of normal passing through point (1, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}) and

slope of normal = {tex}\frac {-1}{m_{(tangent )}} = \sqrt3{/tex}

{tex}(y - \sqrt3) = \sqrt3 (x - 1){/tex}

(y - {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}) = {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}x - {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} ........(iii)
Now, the Eqs. (i) and (ii) can be represented in the graph as shown below:

On putting y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get
x + 0 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the tangent line x + {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}y = 4 cuts the X-axis at A(4,0).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = Area of shaded region OAB
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } y _ { ( \text { equation of normal) } } d x{/tex}+ {tex}\int _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } y _\text{ (equation of tangent) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \sqrt { 3 } x d x + \int _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { 4 - x } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 3 } \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \left[ 4 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 1 } ^ { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \left[ 16 - \frac { 16 } { 2 } - 4 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \left[ 12 - \frac { 15 } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \left[ \frac { 9 } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } + \frac { 3 \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } {/tex}
{tex}= 2 \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}sq units.

explanation: According to the question , Given curves are x - y + 2 = 0....(i) x = {tex}\sqrt y{/tex} ...(ii) Consider x = {tex}\sqrt y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = y, which represents the parabola vertex of parabola is (0, 0) axis of parabola is Y-axis. Now, the point of intersection of Eqs.(i) and (ii) is given by x = {tex}\sqrt{x + 2}{/tex} Squaring on both sides , {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = x + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 - x - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)(x + 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1, 2 When x = -1 , does not satisfy the Eq. (ii). When x = 2 , then 2 = {tex}\sqrt y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4 Hence, the point of intersection is (2, 4). But actual equation of given parabola is x = {tex}\sqrt y{/tex} , it means a semi-parabola which is on right side of Y -axis. The graph of given curves are shown below: Clearly, area of bounded region = Area of region OABO {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left[ y _ \text{ ( line ) } - y _ { ( \text { parabola } ) } \right] d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( x + 2 ) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + 2 x \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - \left[ \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} = {tex}\left[ \frac { 4 } { 2 } + 4 - 0 \right] - \left[ \frac { 8 } { 3 } - 0 \right]{/tex} {tex} = 6 - \frac { 8 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 18 - 8 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{3}{/tex} sq.units.

axis of parabola is Y-axis.

explanation: p>To find area of region bounded by x-axis the ordinates x = -2 and x = 3 and y -1 = x ...(i) Equation (i) is a line that meets at axes at (0, 1) and (-1, 0) A rough sketch of the given information is as under:- Bounded region provides the required area. A = {tex}\int_\limits{-1}^{3} y d x+\left|\int_\limits{-2}^{-1} y d x\right|{/tex}

A = {tex}\frac{17}{2}{/tex} sq. units

question_html: If the area bounded by the parabola {tex}y^{2}=16 a x{/tex} and the line {tex}y=4 m x{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{12}{/tex} sq. units, then using integration, find the value of m.
question_text: If the area bounded by the parabola {tex}y^{2}=16 a x{/tex} and the line {tex}y=4 m x{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{12}{/tex} sq. units, then using integration, find the value of m.
explanation: The given equations are : y 2 = 16ax ...(1) y = 4mx ....(2) Equation (1) represent a parabola having centre at the origin and vertex along positive x−axis. Equation (2) represents a straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of 45 with x−axis. POINTS OF INTERSECTION : Put y = 4mx in (1), we get 16m 2 x 2 − 16ax = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x [m 2 x − a] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0; x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^2}{/tex} When x = 0; y = 0 When x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^{2}}{/tex} , then y = {tex}\frac{4 a}{m}{/tex} Required area =Area under parabola - Area under line = {tex}4 \sqrt{a} \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} \sqrt{x} d x-4 m \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} x d x{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{\mathrm{a}} \times \frac{2}{3}\left[\mathrm{x}^{3 \over 2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}-\frac{4 \mathrm{m}}{2}\left[\mathrm{x}^{2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{2}{3}\frac{a^2}{m^3}{/tex} Now, area = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^{3}=8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} m = 2
solution: The given equations are : y 2 = 16ax ...(1) y = 4mx ....(2) Equation (1) represent a parabola having centre at the origin and vertex along positive x−axis. Equation (2) represents a straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of 45 with x−axis. POINTS OF INTERSECTION : Put y = 4mx in (1), we get 16m 2 x 2 − 16ax = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x [m 2 x − a] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0; x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^2}{/tex} When x = 0; y = 0 When x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^{2}}{/tex} , then y = {tex}\frac{4 a}{m}{/tex} Required area =Area under parabola - Area under line = {tex}4 \sqrt{a} \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} \sqrt{x} d x-4 m \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} x d x{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{\mathrm{a}} \times \frac{2}{3}\left[\mathrm{x}^{3 \over 2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}-\frac{4 \mathrm{m}}{2}\left[\mathrm{x}^{2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{2}{3}\frac{a^2}{m^3}{/tex} Now, area = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^{3}=8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} m = 2
solution_html:
The given equations are :
y2 = 16ax   ...(1)
y = 4mx ....(2)
Equation (1) represent a parabola having centre at the origin and vertex along positive x−axis.
Equation (2) represents a straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of 45 with x−axis.
POINTS OF INTERSECTION :
Put y = 4mx in (1), we get
16m2x2 − 16ax = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x [m2x − a] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0; x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^2}{/tex}
When x = 0; y = 0
When x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^{2}}{/tex}, then y = {tex}\frac{4 a}{m}{/tex}

Required area =Area under parabola - Area under line

= {tex}4 \sqrt{a} \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} \sqrt{x} d x-4 m \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} x d x{/tex}
{tex}=4 \sqrt{\mathrm{a}} \times \frac{2}{3}\left[\mathrm{x}^{3 \over 2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}-\frac{4 \mathrm{m}}{2}\left[\mathrm{x}^{2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{2}{3}\frac{a^2}{m^3}{/tex}
Now, area = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^{3}=8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} m = 2

explanation: To find area of region {tex}\left\{(x, y): \frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4} \leq 1 \leq \frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}\right\}{/tex} Here {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii) Equation (i) represents an ellipse with centre at origin and meets axes at {tex}(\pm 3,0){/tex} {tex}(0, \pm 2){/tex} . Equation (ii) is a line that meets axes at (3, 0) (0, 2). A rough sketch is as under: Bounded region represents required area. This is sliced into rectangles with area (y 1 - y 2 ) {tex}\triangle{/tex} x which slides from x = 0 to x = 3, so Required area of bounded region =Area of the Region APBQA {tex}A=\int_{0}^{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{3}\left[\frac{2}{3} \sqrt{9-x^{2}} d x-\frac{2}{3}(3-x) d x\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{9-x^{2}}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{3}\right)-3 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\left\{0+\frac{9}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}-9+\frac{9}{2}\right\}-\{0\}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{9 \pi}{4}-\frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore A=\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}-3\right){/tex} sq. units
solution: To find area of region {tex}\left\{(x, y): \frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4} \leq 1 \leq \frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}\right\}{/tex} Here {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii) Equation (i) represents an ellipse with centre at origin and meets axes at {tex}(\pm 3,0){/tex} {tex}(0, \pm 2){/tex} . Equation (ii) is a line that meets axes at (3, 0) (0, 2). A rough sketch is as under: Bounded region represents required area. solution_html:
To find area of region
{tex}\left\{(x, y): \frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4} \leq 1 \leq \frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}\right\}{/tex}
Here
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii)
Equation (i) represents an ellipse with centre at origin and meets axes at {tex}(\pm 3,0){/tex} {tex}(0, \pm 2){/tex}.
Equation (ii) is a line that meets axes at (3, 0) (0, 2).
A rough sketch is as under:

Bounded region represents required area. This is sliced into rectangles with area (y1 - y2){tex}\triangle{/tex}x which slides from x = 0 to x = 3, so
Required area of bounded region =Area of the  Region APBQA
{tex}A=\int_{0}^{3}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{3}\left[\frac{2}{3} \sqrt{9-x^{2}} d x-\frac{2}{3}(3-x) d x\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{9-x^{2}}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{3}\right)-3 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{3}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\left\{0+\frac{9}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}-9+\frac{9}{2}\right\}-\{0\}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{9 \pi}{4}-\frac{9}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore A=\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}-3\right){/tex}sq. units

question_html: Find the area of the region in the first quadrant enclosed by the x-axis, the line y = x, and the circle x2 + y2 = 32
question_text: Find the area of the region in the first quadrant enclosed by the x-axis, the line y = x, and the circle x 2 + y 2 = 32
explanation: The given equations are y = x ... (1) and x 2 + y 2 = 32 ... (2) Solving (1) and (2), we find that the line and the circle meet at B(4, 4) in the first quadrant. Draw perpendicular BM to the x-axis. Thus, the required area = area of the region OBMO + area of the region BMAB. Now, the area of the region OBMO {tex}= \int_0^4 {ydx = \int_0^4 {xdx} } {/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{x^2}} \right]_0^4 = 8{/tex} .....(3) Again, the area of the region BMAB {tex}= \int_4^{4\sqrt 2 } {ydx = \int_4^{4\sqrt 2 } {\sqrt {32 - {x^2}} dx} } {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{1}{2}x\sqrt {32 - {x^2}} + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{x}{{4\sqrt 2 }}} \right]_4^{4\sqrt 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \left( {\frac{1}{2}4\sqrt 2 \times 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}1} \right) - \left( {\frac{4}{3}\sqrt {32 - 16} + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right){/tex} {tex}= 8\pi - \left( {8 + 4\pi } \right) = 4\pi - 8{/tex} ..(4) Adding (3) and (4), we get, the required area = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

Which is the required solution.

y = x ... (1)

{tex}= \int_0^4 {ydx = \int_0^4 {xdx} } {/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{x^2}} \right]_0^4 = 8{/tex} .....(3)
Again, the area of the region BMAB

{tex}= \left[ {\frac{1}{2}x\sqrt {32 - {x^2}} + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{x}{{4\sqrt 2 }}} \right]_4^{4\sqrt 2 }{/tex} 
{tex}= \left( {\frac{1}{2}4\sqrt 2 \times 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}1} \right) - \left( {\frac{4}{3}\sqrt {32 - 16} + \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= 8\pi - \left( {8 + 4\pi } \right) = 4\pi - 8{/tex} ..(4)
Adding (3) and (4), we get, the required area = 4{tex}\pi{/tex}. Which is the required solution.

question_text: Using integration, find the area of the region in the first quadrant enclosed by the x-axis, the line y = x and the circle x 2 + y 2 = 18.
explanation: Point of intersection of Circle and Line is (3, 3). {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area = {tex}\int_{0}^{3} x d x+\int_{3}^{3 \sqrt{2}} \sqrt{18-x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{3}+\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{18-x^{2}}+9 \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{3 \sqrt{2}}\right]_{3}^{3 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9}{2}+\frac{9 \pi}{2}-\frac{9}{2}-\frac{9 \pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9}{2}+\frac{9 \pi}{2}-\frac{9}{2}-\frac{9 \pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \pi}{4}{/tex} sq. units.

solution_html:
Point of intersection of Circle and Line is (3, 3).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area = {tex}\int_{0}^{3} x d x+\int_{3}^{3 \sqrt{2}} \sqrt{18-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{3}+\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{18-x^{2}}+9 \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{3 \sqrt{2}}\right]_{3}^{3 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9}{2}+\frac{9 \pi}{2}-\frac{9}{2}-\frac{9 \pi}{4}{/tex}

question_text: Find the area bounded by the curve y -x|x|, x-axis and the ordinates x = - 3 and x = 3. Find the equation of the curve?
explanation: The equation of the curve is y = {tex}x|x|{/tex} = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{c}{x^{2}, x \geq 0} \\ {-x^{2}, x<0}\end{array}\right.{/tex} The graph of y = x {tex}|x|{/tex} is shown in Fig. and the region bounded by y = x {tex}|x|{/tex} , x-axis and the ordinates x = - 3 and x = 3 is shaded in the given Fig. Clearly, y = x|x|, being an odd function is symmetric in opposite quadrants. Therefore, the required area is twice the area of the shaded region in the first quadrant. Let us slice the region in first quadrant into vertical strips. The approximating rectangle shown in Fig. has length = |y 1 |dx. As it can move between x = 0 and x = 3, therefore, area of the shaded region in first quadrant A = {tex}\int_{0}^{3}\left|y_{1}\right| d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{3} y_{1} d x{/tex} .......[ {tex}\because{/tex} y 1 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left|y_{1}\right|{/tex} = y 1 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\int_{0}^{3} x^{2} d x{/tex} ........[ {tex}\because{/tex} P(x 1 , y 1 ) lies on y = x 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} y 1 = x 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{3}{/tex} = 9 sq. units. Hence required area = 2A = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = 18 sq. units.

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{3}{/tex} = 9 sq. units.

question_text: Using the method of integration, find the area of the region bounded by the lines: 2x + y = 4, 3x - 2y = 6 and x - 3y + 5 = 0.
explanation: Equation of one line {tex}{l_1}{/tex} is 2x + y = 4, Equation of second line {tex}{l_2}{/tex} is 3x - 2y = 6 And Equation of third line {tex}{l_3}{/tex} is x - 3y + 5 = 0 Here, vertices of triangle ABC are A (2, 0), B (4, 3) and C (1, 2). Now, Required area of triangle = Area of trapezium CLMB - Area {tex}\triangle ACM{/tex} - Area {tex}\triangle ABL{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\int\limits_1^4 {\frac{1}{3}\left( {x + 5} \right)dx} } \right| - \left| {\int\limits_1^2 {\left( {4 - 2x} \right)dx} } \right| - \left| {\int\limits_2^4 {\frac{3}{2}\left( {x - 2} \right)dx} } \right|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\left| {\left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + 5x} \right)_1^4} \right| - \left| {\left( {4x - \frac{{2{x^2}}}{2}} \right)_1^2} \right| - \frac{3}{2}\left| {\left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - 2x} \right)_2^4} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\left[ {8 + 20 - \left( {\frac{1}{2} + 5} \right)} \right] - \left\{ {\left( {8 - 4} \right) - \left( {4 - 1} \right)} \right\}{/tex} {tex} - \frac{3}{2}\left| {\left( {8 - 8} \right) - \left( {2 - 4} \right)} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\left( {28 - \frac{{11}}{2}} \right) - \left( {4 - 3} \right) - \frac{3}{2} \times 2{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{{45}}{2} - 1 - 3{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{15}}{2} - 1 - 3 = \frac{7}{2}{/tex} sq. units

And Equation of third line {tex}{l_3}{/tex} is x - 3y + 5 = 0

Here, vertices of triangle ABC are A (2, 0), B (4, 3) and C (1, 2).
Now, Required area of triangle
= Area of trapezium CLMB - Area {tex}\triangle ACM{/tex} - Area {tex}\triangle ABL{/tex}

question_text: Sketch the graph of y = Ix + 3I and evaluate the area under the curve y = Ix + 3I above X - axis and between x = - 6 to x = 0.
explanation: First, we sketch the graph of {tex} y = |x + 3|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}y = |x + 3|={/tex} {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { x + 3 , } & { \text { if } \quad x + 3 \geq 0 } \\ { - ( x + 3 ) , } & { \text { if } \quad x + 3 < 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = |x + 3|={/tex} {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { c c c } { x + 3 , } & { \text { if } } & { x \geq - 3 } \\ { - x - 3 , } & { \text { if } } & { x < - 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} So, we have {tex}y = x + 3{/tex} for x {tex}\geq{/tex} - 3 and y = -x - 3 for x < -3 A sketch of {tex}y = |x + 3|{/tex} is shown below: {tex}y = x + 3{/tex} is the straight line which cuts X and Y-axes at (-3, 0 and (0, 3), respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}y = x + 3{/tex} for x {tex}\geq{/tex} -3 represents the part of the line which lies on the right side of x = -3. Similarly, {tex} y = -x - 3{/tex} , x < -3 represents the part of line {tex}y = - x - 3{/tex} , which lies on left side of x = -3 Clearly, required area = Area of region ABPA + Area of region PCOP {tex}= \int _ { - 6 } ^ { - 3 } ( - x - 3 ) d x + \int _ { - 3 } ^ { 0 } ( x + 3 ) d x{/tex} {tex}= \left[ - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 3 x \right] _ { - 6 } ^ { - 3 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + 3 x \right] _ { - 3 } ^ { 0 }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \left( - \frac { 9 } { 2 } + 9 \right) - ( - 18 + 18 ) \right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[ 0 - \left( \frac { 9 } { 2 } - 9 \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left( - \frac { 9 } { 2 } - \frac { 9 } { 2 } \right) + (9 + 9){/tex} = 18 - 9 = 9 sq. units

First, we sketch the graph of {tex} y = |x + 3|{/tex}

 {tex}\Rightarrow y = |x + 3|={/tex}{tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { c c c } { x + 3 , } & { \text { if } } & { x \geq - 3 } \\ { - x - 3 , } & { \text { if } } & { x < - 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
So, we have {tex}y = x + 3{/tex} for x {tex}\geq{/tex} - 3 and y = -x - 3 for x < -3

{tex}y = x + 3{/tex} is the straight line which cuts X and Y-axes at (-3, 0 and (0, 3), respectively.

{tex}= \int _ { - 6 } ^ { - 3 } ( - x - 3 ) d x + \int _ { - 3 } ^ { 0 } ( x + 3 ) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 3 x \right] _ { - 6 } ^ { - 3 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + 3 x \right] _ { - 3 } ^ { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \left( - \frac { 9 } { 2 } + 9 \right) - ( - 18 + 18 ) \right]{/tex}+ {tex}\left[ 0 - \left( \frac { 9 } { 2 } - 9 \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left( - \frac { 9 } { 2 } - \frac { 9 } { 2 } \right) + (9 + 9){/tex}
= 18 - 9
= 9 sq. units

question_text: Using integration, find the area of the following region. {(x, y): Ix - 1I {tex}\leq{/tex} y {tex}\leq \sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} }.
explanation: According to the question , Given region is {tex}{(x, y): |x - 1| \le} \ y \leq \sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Above region has two equations {tex}y = |x - 1|{/tex} and y = {tex} \sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now , {tex} y = |x - 1| =\left\{ \begin{aligned} x - 1 , & \text { if } x - 1 \geq 0 \\ - ( x - 1 ) , & \text { if } x - 1 < 0 \end{aligned} \right.{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \left\{ \begin{array} { c l } {x - 1 , } & { \text { if } x \geq 1 } \\ { 1 - x , } & { \text { if } x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Also, other curve is y = {tex} \sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Squaring on both sides, we get y 2 = 5 - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = 5 which represents equation of circle centre of the circle is (0, 0) radius of circle is = {tex}\sqrt5{/tex} But the actual equation of curve is y = {tex} \sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which represents a semi-circle whose centre is ( 0, 0) and radius r = {tex}\sqrt 5 {/tex} For finding the points of intersection of the curves, we have {tex}y = 1 - x{/tex} ......(i) {tex}y = x - 1{/tex} ......(ii) and y = {tex} \sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .....(iii) On putting y = 1 - x from Eq. (i) in Eq. (iii), we get 1 - x = {tex} \sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Squaring on both sides , {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + (1 - x) 2 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + 1 + x 2 - 2 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 2x - 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 - x - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 - 2x + x - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x - 2) + 1 (x - 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (x - 2) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = - 1 or 2 When x = -1, then y = {tex}\sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 5 - 1 } = \sqrt { 4 } \Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 When x = 2, then y = {tex}\sqrt { 5 - x ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 5 - 4 } = \sqrt { 1 } \Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1 So, points of intersection of Eqs. (i) ad (iii) are (-1, 2) and (2, 1). Similarly, on solving Eq. (ii) and Eq. (iii), we get x = -1 or 2 When x = -1 , then y = 2 When x = 2, then y = 1. question_html: Find the area of the region {tex} \left\{ {(x,y):0 \leqslant y \leqslant ({x^2} + 1),0 \leqslant y \leqslant (x + 1),0 \leqslant x \leqslant 2} \right\}{/tex}
question_text: Find the area of the region {tex} \left\{ {(x,y):0 \leqslant y \leqslant ({x^2} + 1),0 \leqslant y \leqslant (x + 1),0 \leqslant x \leqslant 2} \right\}{/tex}
explanation: There are three curves respectively, C 1 = {(x, y): {tex} 0\leq y \leq x^2+1{/tex} } C 2 = {(x, y): {tex} 0\leq y\leq x+1{/tex} } C 3 ={(x,y): {tex} 0\leq x\leq 2{/tex} } The points of intersection of, y = x 2 and y = x + 1y are A(0,1) and B(1,2). The required area of shaded region = {tex} \int_0^1y_1 dx+\int_1^2 y_2dx{/tex} where y 1 = x 2 + 1 and y 2 = x + 1 {tex}\therefore A=\int_0^1(x^2+1)dx+\int_1^2(x+1)dx{/tex} {tex}=[\frac{x^3}{3}+x]_0^1+[\frac{x^2}{2}+x]_1^2{/tex} {tex} =(\frac{4}{3}-0)+(4-\frac{3}{2})=\frac{8+24-9}{6}=\frac{23}{6}\ sq.units{/tex}
solution: There are three curves respectively, C 1 = {(x, y): {tex} 0\leq y \leq x^2+1{/tex} } C 2 = {(x, y): {tex} 0\leq y\leq x+1{/tex} } C 3 ={(x,y): {tex} 0\leq x\leq 2{/tex} } The points of intersection of, y = x 2 and y = x + 1y are A(0,1) and B(1,2). The required area of shaded region = {tex} \int_0^1y_1 dx+\int_1^2 y_2dx{/tex} where y 1 = x 2 + 1 and y 2 = x + 1 {tex}\therefore A=\int_0^1(x^2+1)dx+\int_1^2(x+1)dx{/tex} {tex}=[\frac{x^3}{3}+x]_0^1+[\frac{x^2}{2}+x]_1^2{/tex} {tex} =(\frac{4}{3}-0)+(4-\frac{3}{2})=\frac{8+24-9}{6}=\frac{23}{6}\ sq.units{/tex}

There are three curves respectively,
C1 = {(x, y):{tex} 0\leq y \leq x^2+1{/tex}}

C3 ={(x,y):{tex} 0\leq x\leq 2{/tex}}
The points of intersection of, y = x2 and y = x + 1y are A(0,1) and B(1,2).
The required area of shaded region = {tex} \int_0^1y_1 dx+\int_1^2 y_2dx{/tex} 
where y1 = x2 + 1 and y2 = x + 1
{tex}\therefore A=\int_0^1(x^2+1)dx+\int_1^2(x+1)dx{/tex} 
{tex}=[\frac{x^3}{3}+x]_0^1+[\frac{x^2}{2}+x]_1^2{/tex} 
{tex} =(\frac{4}{3}-0)+(4-\frac{3}{2})=\frac{8+24-9}{6}=\frac{23}{6}\ sq.units{/tex}

question_html: Find the area bounded by the circle x2 + y2 = 16 and the line y = x in the first quadrant.
question_text: Find the area bounded by the circle x 2 + y 2 = 16 and the line y = x in the first quadrant.
explanation: The given circle is x 2 + y 2 =16 ...(i) The given line is y = x ...(ii) Putting y = x from (ii) into (i), we get 2x 2 = 16 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 8 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} x is +ve in the first quad.} Thus, the point of intersection of (i) and (ii) in the first quadrant is A {tex}(2 \sqrt{2}, 2 \sqrt{2}){/tex} Draw AL perpendicular on the x-axis ​Therefore required area of region = (area of region OLA) + area of region(LBAL). {tex}=\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}}+\left[\frac{x \sqrt{16-x^{2}}}{2}+\frac{16}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{4}\right]_{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}-0\right]+\left[\left(0+8 \sin ^{-1} 1\right)-\left(4+8 \sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]\right.{/tex} {tex}=\left[4+\left(8 \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right)-4-\left(8 \times \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]{/tex} = (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ) sq units.
solution: The given circle is x 2 + y 2 =16 ...(i) The given line is y = x ...(ii) Putting y = x from (ii) into (i), we get 2x 2 = 16 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 8 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} x is +ve in the first quad.} Thus, the point of intersection of (i) and (ii) in the first quadrant is A {tex}(2 \sqrt{2}, 2 \sqrt{2}){/tex} Draw AL perpendicular on the x-axis ​Therefore required area of region = (area of region OLA) + area of region(LBAL). {tex}=\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}}+\left[\frac{x \sqrt{16-x^{2}}}{2}+\frac{16}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{4}\right]_{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}-0\right]+\left[\left(0+8 \sin ^{-1} 1\right)-\left(4+8 \sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]\right.{/tex} {tex}=\left[4+\left(8 \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right)-4-\left(8 \times \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]{/tex} = (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ) sq units.
solution_html:
The given circle is x2 + y2 =16 ...(i)
The given line is y = x ...(ii)

Putting y = x from (ii) into (i), we get
2x2 = 16 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 = 8 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} x is  +ve in the first quad.}
Thus, the point of intersection of (i) and (ii) in the first quadrant is A{tex}(2 \sqrt{2}, 2 \sqrt{2}){/tex}
Draw AL perpendicular on the x-axis
​Therefore required area of region = (area of region OLA) + area of region(LBAL). 
{tex}=\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}}+\left[\frac{x \sqrt{16-x^{2}}}{2}+\frac{16}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{4}\right]_{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}-0\right]+\left[\left(0+8 \sin ^{-1} 1\right)-\left(4+8 \sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]\right.{/tex}
{tex}=\left[4+\left(8 \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right)-4-\left(8 \times \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]{/tex} = (2{tex}\pi{/tex}) sq units.

question_html: Using integration, find the area of the region hounded by the line 2y + x = 8, the x-axis and the lines x = 2 and x = 4.
question_text: Using integration, find the area of the region hounded by the line 2y + x = 8, the x-axis and the lines x = 2 and x = 4.
explanation: The given line AB is 2y + x = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4 - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} x ...(i) and axis is x = 2, and x = 4, Required area = area PLMQP = area between the line y = 4 - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} x, and the x-axis between x = 2 and x = 4 {tex}=\int_\limits{2}^{4} y_{A B} d x=\int_\limits{2}^{4}\left(4-\frac{1}{2} x\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[4 x-\frac{1}{4} x^{2}\right]_{2}^{4}{/tex} = (12 - 7) sq units = 5 sq units Hence, the required area is 5 sq units.
